data.title: UDOW: Benefits And Risks Of The 3x Leveraged Dow Jones ETF
data.description:
Fast facts about the ProShares UltraPro Dow30 ETF

The ProShares UltraPro Dow30 ETF (UDOW) is a leveraged ETF based on the Dow Jones Industrial Average Index with a factor of 3x. UDOW was launched on February 9, 2010, has a net expense ratio of 0.95%, and a trailing 12-month yield of 1.31%. It is a mid-size but very liquid ETF, with $656 million of AUM (assets under management) and an average daily dollar volume of $270 million. It means over 40% of AUM changes hands on average in a trading day, pointing to the fund’s popularity among short-term traders. The issuer, ProShares, specializes in leveraged and inverse ETFs, with about one hundred of these types of products based on equity indexes, bond indexes, commodities, currencies and cryptos.

Strategy

UDOW has the objective to provide, before fees and expenses, three times the daily performance of the Dow Jones Industrial Average Index, which is also the underlying index of SPDR® Dow Jones Industrial Average ETF Trust (DIA). ProShares gives a warning on UDOW:

If the Index approaches a 33% loss at any point in the day, you could lose your entire investment. (...)

The performance of the Fund for periods longer than a single day will likely differ from the Daily Target. This difference may be significant.

Indeed, the daily leverage factor generates drift, a topic that I will discuss in this article.

The fund invests in instruments that, in combination, provide 3X daily leveraged exposure to the underlying index, including stocks, swaps and futures related to the index. The fund also holds cash instruments such as Treasury bills and repurchase agreements as collateral. Keeping a daily objective requires to rebalance the portfolio on a daily basis. ProShares doesn’t disclose the fund’s turnover rate on cash instruments and derivatives.

Underlying index

As described by S&P Global, eligible stocks must be part of the S&P 500 Index, excluding those classified in the Utilities sector (GICS code 55) or in the Transportation industry (GICS code 2030).

The index is a 30-stock, price-weighted index that measures the performance of some of the largest U.S. companies.

Constituents are selected by the Index Committee and changed "on an as-needed basis". There is no periodic reconstitution. The Committee doesn’t follow quantitative rules, although it considers a company’s reputation, growth, market interest, the price ratio between the highest-priced stock and the lowest-priced stock, and sector representation. The index is rebalanced semi-annually after the close of the third Friday of March and September. The turnover is low, for instance, 3% in the most recent fiscal year.

The top 10 issuers, listed in the next table based on DIA holdings, represent 54.7% of asset value and the top three names (Goldman Sachs, Caterpillar and Microsoft) weigh between 6% and 11%.

The Dow Jones Industrial Average has a focus on financials (27% of asset value) and information technology (20.6%), while other sectors are below 15%.

Compared to the S&P 500 Index, represented in the next table by SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY), the Dow Jones Industrial Average is cheaper based on the P/E ratio and has lower earnings growth and cash flow growth rates.

Source: Fidelity

UDOW Portfolio

The leveraged fund has 74.8% of its asset value directly invested in the stocks of the underlying index. Swap contracts issued by six banks have an aggregate notional weight of 217.4%, and Dow Jones Industrial Average futures represent 7.8% of exposure. The total of 300% corresponds to the leverage factor.

Diversification in several swap issuers reduces counterparty risks and dependency. They are: UBS AG, SOCIÉTÉ GÉNÉRALE, BNP PARIBAS, BARCLAYS, CITIBANK, and BANK OF AMERICA.

Weights and metrics are given as an example from 11/19/2025 and may have changed by the time you read this.

Performance

UDOW has delivered 5.9 times the total return of DIA from 2/16/2010 to 11/19/2025 (through multiple splits), with a maximum drawdown of -80.3%, as reported in the table below. The ratio of annualized returns in this period is close to 2.0, below the daily leverage factor.

from 2/16/10

Variability in the ratio points to path-dependent drift. Additionally, the Sharpe ratio of UDOW (a measure of risk-adjusted performance) is inferior to that of the unleveraged ETF on all time frames.

Why and how do leveraged ETFs drift?

Leveraged ETFs often underperform their underlying index leveraged by the same factor. ETF decay may have four reasons: beta slippage, roll yield, tracking errors, and management costs. Beta slippage is the main reason in equity leveraged ETFs such as UDOW. To understand it, imagine a very volatile asset that goes up 25% one day and down 20% the day after. A perfect 2X leveraged ETF goes up 50% the first day and down 40% the second day. At the close of the second day, the underlying asset is back to its initial price:

(1 + 0.25) x (1 - 0.2) = 1.

At the same time, the perfectly leveraged ETF has lost 10%:

(1 + 0.5) x (1 - 0.4) = 0.9.

It is just the result of compounding leveraged returns. In a trending market, beta slippage can be positive. If the underlying index goes up 10% two days in a row, on the second day, it is up 21%:

(1 + 0.1) x (1 + 0.1) = 1.21.

The perfect 2X leveraged ETFs are up 44%:

(1 + 0.2) x (1 + 0.2) = 1.44.

Beta slippage is path-dependent. If the underlying index gains 50% on day 1 and loses 33.33% on day 2, it is back to its initial value, like in the first example. However, the 2X ETF loses one-third of its value instead of 10% in the first case:

(1 + 1) x (1 - 0.6667) = 0.6667.

With this demonstration, it shows that the higher the volatility, the larger the decay.

Moreover, the higher the leverage factor, the larger the drift. If we come back to the first example of this section, a perfect 3X ETF would gain 75% on day 1 and lose 60% on day 2:

(1 + 0.75) x (1 - 0.6) = 0.7.

The 3X ETF is down 30% in two days, while the 2X ETF is down only 10% and the underlying index is unchanged.

Drift calculation and history

There is no standard definition for the drift of a leveraged ETF. Mine is simple and based on the difference between the leveraged ETF performance and Ñ times the performance of the underlying index over a given time interval, if Ñ is the leveraging factor. First, let’s start by defining “Return”: it is the total return of a leveraged ETF in a given time interval. “Index Return” is the total return of a non-leveraged ETF on the same underlying asset in the same time interval. “Abs” is the absolute value operator. “Drift” is the drift of a leveraged ETF normalized to the underlying index exposure in a time interval, calculated as follows:

Drift = (Return - (Index Return x Ñ))/ Abs(Ñ)

The next table reports monthly and annual drifts of DOW and its bear counterpart, ProShares UltraPro Short Dow30 ETF (SDOW).

Ticker

Ñ

1-month Return

1-month Drift

1-year Return

1-year Drift

DIA

1

-1.22%

0.00%

7.04%

0.00%

UDOW

3

-4.74%

-0.36%

0.63%

-6.83%

SDOW

-3

4.21%

0.18%

-18.49%

0.88%

UDOW has a moderate negative drift in both time frames. The next chart plots the 12-month drift of UDOW from 2/16/2011 to 11/19/2025 (to match the inception date and look-back period).

The 12-month drift has a negative historical average of -2.38%.

Related ETFs

There is no other ETF listed in the U.S. with the same underlying index and the same leverage factor. Nonetheless, the following leveraged ETFs with different factors are based on the Dow Jones Industrial Average:

  • ProShares UltraPro Short Dow30 ETF (SDOW).
  • ProShares Ultra Dow30 ETF (DDM).
  • ProShares UltraShort Dow30 ETF (DXD).
  • ProShares Short Dow30 ETF (DOG).

The next table compares their characteristics.

UDOW

SDOW

DDM

DXD

DOG

Leverage

3

-3

2

-2

-1

Inception

02/09/2010

02/09/2010

06/19/2006

07/11/2006

06/19/2006

Expense Ratio

0.95%

0.95%

0.95%

0.95%

AUM

$656.16M

$221.51M

$139.81M

Avg Daily Dollar Volume

$270.48M

$22.12M

$31.14M

$48.75M

UDOW has the same expense ratio as the 2x ETF DDM and offers higher liquidity to traders.

DDM has an equivalent mutual fund, ProFunds Ultra Dow 30 Fund Investor Class (UDPIX), but the higher expense ratio of UDPIX (1.61%) makes it quite unattractive.

Takeaway: Who should consider UDOW?

UDOW is an instrument designed for traders executing strategies with clear entry and exit signals and who understand the fund’s behavior behind the leverage factor. Holding UDOW over a long period may work, but historical data underline the risk of drift and deep drawdowns. For investors willing to buy or hold UDOW, a reasonable approach is to stick to an active trading strategy backtested in all market conditions. Monitoring the drift regime and the underlying index volatility can also help decide when to adopt a risk-off stance.

This article answers these three main questions about UDOW:

  1. Is UDOW meant for short-term trading or long-term investing?
  2. Are there unique risks to investing in UDOW?
  3. What type of investor is UDOW best suited for?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: BILL Holdings: Standalone Or Acquired, Investors Can Benefit From Cheap Multiples
data.description:
With the stock market appearing to take a risk-off attitude as we near the end of the year, it’s a great time for investors to rebalance our portfolios to emphasize value (and “growth at a reasonable price”) rather than double down on winning large-cap growth stocks. In particular, I’m focused on small- and mid-cap names that have their own catalysts to outperform the S&P 500.

BILL Holdings (BILL), the payments software company, is a great candidate in this regard. The stock has been pummeled this year due to macro concerns, which particularly hammers the company’s exposure to a slowdown in SMB business activity and payments. But while we certainly acknowledge there are risks to consider, the stock already prices these risks in and then some: Bill.com has crashed ~40% this year to multiyear lows.

I last wrote a "Buy" article on Bill.com in August, when the stock was trading at $48 per share. Since then, Bill.com has fallen slightly further, which is opportune timing as potential M&A rumors pick up steam. The company has still managed to achieve double-digit growth in revenue and total payment volumes (TPV), leading me to reiterate my "Buy" rating on this company.

Before we dig into Bill.com’s latest fundamental trends, it’s worth commenting on the possibility of Bill.com being subject to an M&A offer. Rumors have been circulating that Bill.com has hired bankers to explore a sale, and this is a highly plausible exit scenario for the company given the very acquisitive environment in enterprise software this year. Just this week, Adobe (ADBE) purchased SEO software company Semrush (SEMR) for just shy of 4x revenue. Earlier this year, major PE firm Thoma Bravo snapped up Dayforce. These acquisitions all have a common thread with Bill.com: underappreciated valuation multiples despite strong recurring revenue.

But in my view, whether Bill.com stands to benefit from an outright sale, investors have a tremendous opportunity here to pick up the stock at a sharp discount. At current share prices near $48, Bill.com trades at a market cap of $4.87 billion. After we net off the $2.22 billion of cash and $1.87 billion of debt on the company’s latest balance sheet, Bill.com’s resulting enterprise value is $4.52 billion.

For the current year, FY26 (which ends in September 2026), Bill.com has guided to $1.59-$1.63 billion in revenue, or 9%-11% growth on 12%-15% growth in “core revenue” (which is software revenue that excludes the headwind from float interest). It’s also guiding to a midpoint 16.5% operating margin (+10bps y/y versus 16.4% margin in FY25), which is a notable increase from a previous 15.8% margin expectation and a reflection of careful expense discipline when we consider the revenue headwind from lower float interest rates.

Applying a 10bps y/y bump to Bill.com’s FCF margins would indicate a 21.5% FCF margin and $346.4 million in FCF. This positions Bill.com’s valuation multiples at:

  • 2.8x EV/FY26 revenue
  • 13.0x EV/FY27 FCF

We note that it’s incredibly rare for a company that is still growing to trade at a low teens multiple of FCF. We note as well that Bill.com is trading one turn lower than where Semrush was just acquired, and arguably Semrush had more secular headwinds working against it (AI reducing the need for web search SEO) than Bill.com’s merely cyclical interest rate and macro risks.

Stay long here and use the dip as a buying opportunity in this stock.

Q1 download

Let’s now go through Bill.com’s latest Q1 (September quarter) results in greater detail. The Q1 earnings summary is shown below:

Bill.com's revenue grew 10% y/y to $395.7 million, beating Wall Street's expectations of $391.2 million (+9% y/y) by a one-point margin. Revenue from core subscriptions grew at a faster 14% y/y pace, which was largely in line with 15% y/y growth in Q2. The headwind came from net interest income, which fell 13% y/y due to lower short-term interest rates.

Despite the sharp decay in Bill.com's share price, core metrics for the business still remain quite healthy. Bill.com added 4.7k net-new customers in the quarter, bringing its total to 498.5k customers (+5% y/y)—despite the macroeconomic headwinds that tend to impact SMBs and drive small business failures to a larger degree than enterprise-oriented software companies. Also, the total payment volume processed on the Bill.com network hit a record of $89.3 billion, up 12% y/y.

Key to the company's success is a continuous rollout of new products, especially geared toward more upmarket clients, as Bill.com looks to diversify its revenue stream away from SMB. The company recently released a product called Supplier Payment Plus, geared toward the company's largest supplier customers. Though this product has longer sales cycles, it will help Bill.com build out a fuller end-to-end payments platform for more complex enterprise needs. And on the mid-market side, Bill.com notes that average card spending per customer is also rising to record highs.

Per President John Rettig's remarks on the Q1 earnings call:

SPP is designed for the largest suppliers in our diversified 2-sided network and advances how we serve them by unifying payments, workflows and dedicated account management into a comprehensive purpose-built offering [...]

As with many enterprise solutions, the sales cycle is longer than our historical SMB trials, and we are building out our enterprise go-to-market capabilities to accelerate prospect conversion. Shifting to our progress in mid-market. Our Spend & Expense solution continued strong traction with higher spend businesses who typically have a large volume of transactions and complex business rules. In Q1, card spend per customer reached a record high of $145,000. In support of these larger businesses, we recently introduced a set of new functionality that enable businesses to integrate with their vendors and automatically collect, match and categorize receipts.

And on the profitability side, despite the headwinds from a decline in interest income (which represents "free" margin for the company), Bill.com still managed to slightly grow pro forma operating income to $68.2 million, or a 17.2% pro forma operating margin that is down 150bps y/y.

We note that guidance calls for just a 16.5% pro forma operating margin this year. With the latest Fed minutes potentially signaling for a slower pace of rate cuts, Bill.com may actually end up exceeding its revenue and profit guidance if rates don't drop as sharply as currently anticipated.

Key takeaways

In my view, Bill.com checks off many boxes: great valuation, reliable ~10% y/y growth in revenue, continued customer growth with an effort to land more upmarket customers, and the possibility for an M&A premium. Stay long here and utilize the dip as a buying opportunity.

data.title: Kanzhun: Still Positive Considering Q3 Beat And Sustained Growth
data.description:
My "Buy" rating for Kanzhun Limited (BZ) stays unchanged. I've taken into account the above-consensus 3rd quarter results and encouraging Q4 guidance in making this decision.

I touched on BZ's 2Q25 outperformance and its return of surplus capital to stockholders in the previous Aug. 22, 2025, write-up.

Quarterly Earnings Beat The Consensus Forecast

The group's financials for Jul-Sep '25 were revealed in a press release issued on Wednesday, Nov 19. BZ saw its bottom-line expansion accelerate from 2Q2025's +31% to 3Q25's +34% on a year-on-year basis.

Its third-quarter net profit amounting to CNY 0.99 billion was 6.6% above the sell-side's estimate, as per S&P Capital IQ.

BZ's strong showing was driven by both revenue improvement and profitability enhancement.

Turnover for the firm was 13% YoY higher at CNY 2.16 billion in the latest three-month period. That was better than its 2Q25 sales increase of 9%.

My late-Aug piece noted that "Kanzhun's top-line performance was previously hurt by an imbalance between the Mainland Chinese employment market's demand and supply" which "has become less of a problem." This thesis is still true.

According to BZ's results announcement, its 4Q24-3Q25 "paid enterprise customers" and 3Q2025 "average monthly active users" increased 13.3% and 10.0%, respectively, on YoY terms.

The company's analyst briefing remarks also disclosed that "the average number of daily active enterprise users grew at a faster pace sequentially than job seekers (in 3Q25), marking the first time this has happened in 3 years." This implies that the rise in prospective employees exceeded the increase in available positions on Kanzhun's platform for an extended period before the third quarter of the current year.

The favorable change in demand-supply dynamics has been good for its recent revenues.

Moving on to margins, BZ's EBIT-to-sales improved from 31.7% in 3Q24 to 41.8% for 3Q2025.

It has been managing its "Sales & Marketing/S&M" expenses well.

The 3Q25 S&M-to-turnover metric declined by 9.7ppts (percentage points) to 19.1%. In absolute terms, S&M-related costs fell 25% to CNY 0.39 billion in the quarter.

BZ explained in the earnings release that it's "benefiting from enhanced marketing efficiency driven by the platform’s strong network effects."

Anticipating A Similarly Rapid Growth Rate In Q4

The firm is guiding for sales of CNY 2,060 million (mid-point) in the final quarter of this year.

This is equivalent to a 13% increase, or the same as that for 3Q2025.

In my opinion, it can meet or even surpass the management outlook, considering key indicators presented below.

BZ's "4Q25 quarter-to-date iOS grossing was +23% yoy versus +19% yoy in 3Q25." The information was taken from Morgan Stanley's (MS) Nov. 17, 2025, research note (inaccessible in public) with the title "Chart Of The Day: iOS Grossing."

Its "In-App Purchase" or "IAP" income is growing quicker in Q4 thus far vis-a-vis Q3.

Also, Kanzhun's industry penetration rate is still reasonably modest at the low teens percent level. Its number of "paid enterprise customers" was 6.8 million for the last 12 months.

An Aug. 10, 2025, article published by Chinese state media "Global Times" stated that "as of the end of 2024, China had more than 60 million SMEs (small and medium-sized enterprises)."

Separately, BZ highlighted on the results call that it has witnessed "a potential turning point from previous downward trajectory." The group's optimism is supported by the fact that its "net dollar retention rate started to bottom out" for "the first time in the past 2 years."

Earlier, Kanzhun's corporate clients had been hesitant to extend their existing agreements relating to the former's recruitment platform solutions. Things are changing in a positive manner, as mentioned above.

You Should Consider These Worst-Case Scenarios

A supply-demand imbalance in China's jobs market will be bad for BZ. This can happen if enterprises prioritize cost-cutting and there's an influx of new university graduates in a particular academic year.

The Chinese recruitment industry's offline-to-online transition may occur at a slower-than-anticipated pace. That will affect Kanzhun's growth prospects.

My Concluding Thoughts

I'm still a bull on BZ.

I compare this name with "Recruit Holdings (OTCPK:RCRUY) (OTCPK:RCRRF) and SEEK Limited (OTCPK:SKLTY) (OTCPK:SKLTF)", as I've done in previous write-ups.

The consensus FY26 P/E multiples for BZ, RCRUY, and SKLTY are 17x, 25x, and 44x (source: S&P Capital IQ), respectively.

Kanzhun's capital appreciation potential is 47% (25/17) benchmarked with RCRRF.

I wrote in the Aug 22 update that "BZ's sales growth has moderated for six quarters in a row since Q1'24." My take at that time was "a potential reversal of this negative trend" can "act as the stock's re-rating trigger."

BZ's 4Q2025 results are expected to be released in March next year. The continuation of top-line growth in the teens percentage range should justify a more demanding valuation.

data.title: Pitney Bowes Leans On Cost Cuts As Shrinking Sales Limit Its Comeback
data.description:
Looking back at my last Hold rating for Pitney Bowes Inc. (PBI), not much has changed to make me want to move off that neutral stance. The last few months have been rough. The stock fell from $11.16 to $9.48. That’s about a 15% drop and gives back much of its recent rally. Investors reacted to another quarter where Pitney Bowes did some things right, like getting costs under control and making the business leaner but the same big problems are still hanging around. Sales keep dropping and the company owes a lot of money. Even though profits and cash flow are better, nothing has really shifted enough to make me say this is a great time to buy or a must-sell, either. To me, the market sees all this and is pricing the stock about right. There aren’t any big surprises or bargains here right now.

Why am I sticking with Hold rating? First, the company stopped doing e-commerce and is now focusing on its main businesses, mailing technology and mail sorting for big customers. That’s helped boost profit margins, which is good. Second, revenue just won’t stop shrinking. This year, sales are on track to fall about 8% and next year looks flat or maybe a little worse. Third, debt is a real weight. Pitney Bowes still owes more than $2 billion, with only about $320 million in cash, so it doesn’t have much breathing room.

Management’s aiming for about $330 million in free cash flow this year. They bumped up the stock buyback again and even raised the dividend three times in 2025. But honestly, that feels more like a move to keep investors from getting nervous rather than a sign that growth is coming back. Unless they can find a way to make sales steady again, I just don’t see the stock moving up much. Waiting is the only real option for now.

Business Snapshot

Pitney Bowes is a simpler company than it was even a year ago. The old e-commerce business is gone. Now they sell mailing machines, shipping software and services to small and mid-sized businesses and they also help big clients sort their mail to get postal discounts. The company is running more efficiently, cutting costs and bringing in some new leaders to help turn things around. But, in reality, things are still hard. SendTech’s sales dropped 6% last quarter and Presort’s sales fell 11% because of lost customers and the fact that people just send less mail now. The business is cleaner and more focused but it’s fighting against a shrinking market.

Market Context

That’s really the heart of the problem. The industries Pitney Bowes works in aren’t growing. Mail volumes keep shrinking year after year. Even though some small businesses still need mailing tools, it’s not enough to make up for the big losses elsewhere. Presort, which sorts mail for discounts, faces the same problem, especially after losing customers when old management set prices too high. There’s some chance things could get better as more companies outsource mail jobs or if Pitney Bowes comes up with better digital products. But right now, their advantage over rivals is shrinking and there’s not much sign they’re about to win big market share. Unless Pitney Bowes can get a bigger piece of the digital side of things, it’s going to be hard to grow.

Financials

If you just look at the numbers, Pitney Bowes is doing well with the things it can control. Profit margins are up. They made $52 million in profit last quarter, a big improvement from last year’s loss. Free cash flow is stabilizing, though still roughly flat year over year. But the company still owes a lot of money and interest costs are weighing on profits. The new debt they raised gives them some breathing room now but it means possible dilution for shareholders in the future. Segment by segment, SendTech is declining mid-single digits year over year and Presort is shrinking. Unless sales stop falling, it’s hard to see profits or debt getting much better.

Valuation

On valuation, the stock trades at about 7 times expected earnings. That sounds cheap, but the business isn’t really growing, so it’s not as great a bargain as it looks. Analysts expect earnings to go up just a little over the next couple years but sales are expected to be flat or down. The market cap is pretty much where it was earlier this year, mainly because the company has bought back a substantial number of shares this year. That means management is supporting the stock, but it also shows the market is waiting for real operational change before rewarding Pitney Bowes with a higher valuation. The stock is cheap for a reason, and unless something changes, I don’t see a big opportunity here.

Sentiment

People aren’t as excited about Pitney Bowes as they were when the new CEO took over and the company started raising dividends. Last quarter, the company didn’t meet expectations for sales or profits. The stock slipped about 11%. That’s probably because there weren’t any good surprises. Some investors like that the dividend is higher and profits are a little better. But most are worried about sales dropping and all the debt. In my view, most people are focused on what already happened, not what’s next. Until the company proves it can actually grow, investors will stay careful.

Risks

The biggest risk is simple: people keep sending less mail and that hurts every part of the business. If that speeds up, or the post office changes its rules, sales could drop even more. If the company’s plan to fix things goes wrong or they lose more big customers, profits could really get hurt. The debt is a big worry, too. If interest rates go up or they bring in less cash, they could find themselves in trouble. The new convertible notes might also mean shareholders end up owning less in the future. If the management team finds new ways to cut costs or grow, maybe I’m being too cautious but right now I see just as many risks as possible wins.

Outlook

Looking ahead, there aren’t many easy ways for Pitney Bowes to grow. More cost-cutting, a little help from their software and maybe they can slowly pay off some debt. Most experts think sales will stay flat or fall a bit and any profit growth will come from cutting costs, not selling more. If the customer base for SendTech stops shrinking, or Presort wins back some business, that’s a good sign. But until that happens, I’m not expecting much. Sure, management could pull off a surprise, or things could get worse but really, we just have to wait and see.

Scenarios

If things go really badly, sales keep falling, profits get worse, cash dries up and the company might need to cut the dividend or take out new loans at worse terms. The stock could fall under $8. If things stay about the same, the stock will probably bounce between $8 and $11. The dividend and buybacks will help but not much else will change. If things go really well, sales stop falling or even go up, maybe because of a new product or a better market and the stock could reach $13 or more. But for that to happen, Pitney Bowes has to show steady growth and pay off more debt.

Final Take

So I’m sticking with Hold. I think Pitney Bowes is doing what it can but the big problems are still here. The market knows it, too. There’s no big bargain but also no reason to sell everything and get out. If you already own the stock, it makes sense to hold and see if things get better. But if you’re thinking about buying or selling now, I’d wait until there’s a clear sign things are really changing.

data.title: Plains All American: Buy This 9% Yield While The Market Ignores Value
data.description:
The ‘AI-trade’ appears to be back in force, with NVIDIA (NVDA) reporting strong fiscal Q4 results. That could extend the valuation gap between tech stocks and value stocks in the current bifurcated market. That’s fine with me, as I’m happy to accumulate high-yielding dividend stocks, as I’m focused more on a return of capital rather than a return on capital.

This brings me to Plains All American Pipeline, L.P. Common Units (PAA), which I last covered in September, highlighting its strategic focus on its core crude oil business, synergies from bolt-on acquisitions, and attractive valuation. At the current price of $16.95, PAA remains in a value sweet spot in the bottom half of its 52-week range with a 9% yield, as shown below.

In this article, I revisit PAA, including recent business results, and discuss why PAA is a solid high-yielding choice for potentially strong total returns from here, so let’s dive in!

Why PAA?

Plains All American issues a Schedule K-1 and is a North American energy midstream company. Its assets include gathering, transportation, storage, and NGL logistics across the U.S. and Canada. PAA will be divesting its NGL assets in Canada early next year to Keyera (OTCPK:KEYUF) in a $3.75 billion deal. Thus, it will be focused primarily on crude oil logistics while retaining its U.S.-based NGL assets.

As shown below, 55% of PAA’s EBITDA comes from the Permian Basin, with the rest evenly spread across Canada/Rocky Mountain, South Texas/Gulf Coast, and Mid-Continent. As shown below, PAA has steadily grown its EBITDA since 2023 despite volatility in oil prices over this timeframe.

PAA recently demonstrated solid Q3 2025 results, with total adjusted EBITDA of $669 million. This includes the Crude Oil segment adjusted EBITDA of $593 million, which grew by $13MM and $16MM on a QoQ and YoY basis. This was driven by higher tariff volumes, contributions from bolt-on acquisitions, and the benefit of annual tariff escalations. As shown below, PAA’s crude oil volumes coming out of the Permian Basin has steadily grown over the past 2 years.

PAA is taking steps to bolster its crude oil business, as reflected in its acquisition of the remaining 45% stake in the EPIC pipeline in November. Management expects Q4’25 to be a baseline for crude oil volumes, with expectations for continued growth in the segment next year. EPIC will be renamed to Cactus III as it’s integrated with PAA’s other pipelines, resulting in synergies.

This transaction is expected to generate an unlevered IRR in the mid-teens. As shown below, this pipeline provides PAA with valuable wellhead-to-water export exposure. This new system gives PAA leverage to expand capacity and take advantage of egress-tight markets.

Looking ahead, PAA is well positioned after the EPIC acquisition, as it now controls a long-haul pipeline system that complement its existing footprint. Management expects much of the cost synergies to be immediate, with capture set to begin in 2026. Plus, EPIC is one of the more planned bolt-on acquisitions as PAA seeks to deploy its proceeds from the NGL sale proceeds. This fits with the strategy of an expanding asset base that reduces PAA’s commodity price exposure, thereby resulting in steady cash flows.

PAA is well-supported by a strong balance sheet with BBB and equivalent credit ratings from S&P, Moody’s, and Fitch. This is reflected by its solid net debt to EBITDA ratio of 3.3x, sitting well under the 4.5x level generally considered safe for midstream companies.

Importantly, the distribution remains well-covered at a DCF-to-distribution coverage ratio of 1.6x. While this has reached management’s target, it’s worth noting that this doesn’t represent the true run rate, as PAA has yet to fully deploy the sale proceeds from the Canadian NGL assets. As such, PAA could be on track to raise the distribution again as funds are deployed into income-generating assets and as DCF coverage rises.

Lastly, I continue to find PAA to be appealing at the current price of $16.95 with a price-to-cash flow ratio of 4.16x. As shown below, this sits in the middle of PAA’s 5-year range and on the low end since the start of 2024.

PAA is also noticeably cheaper than its peers, Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), MPLX LP (MPLX), Energy Transfer (ET), and Western Midstream (WES), which carry P/CF ratios in the 5.4x to 8.9x range, as shown below. Plus, with a 9% distribution yield that’s well covered by cash flow, PAA could support strong total returns from here.

Risks to PAA include potential for spills in environmentally sensitive areas, which could result in costly clean-up. Plus, a slowdown in Permian production could result in lower volume-related earnings. Other risks include commodity price volatility and tariff-related risks, which could affect the oil export market coming out of the Gulf Coast.

Investor Takeaway

Plains All American stands out as an undervalued high-yield midstream ‘Buy’. It has a 9% distribution yield that’s well covered, along with improving fundamentals and a clear path to stronger cash flow. The EPIC acquisition strengthens its crude oil network, adds valuable export exposure, and should drive meaningful synergies once integrated.

Lastly, PAA offers an appealing setup for income investors who want steady assets and the potential for solid total returns. This is supported by a unit price still sitting in value territory and trading at a discount to peers on cash flow. As such, I maintain a ‘Strong Buy’ rating on the units.

data.title: Microsoft Slips As Technical Pressure Outweighs New Saudi Cloud Partnership
data.description:
By Parshwa Turakhiya

​Microsoft (MSFT) shares closed near $487 on Wednesday, extending a steady multi-session decline as the stock continues to unwind from its early-November peak. The weakness came even as the company announced a memorandum of understanding with Saudi Arabia’s Public Investment Fund and SITE to explore sovereign-cloud services in the kingdom.

While the strategic partnership signals continued expansion of Microsoft’s global cloud footprint, the market’s response has been muted. Technical pressures, broader Nasdaq softness, and renewed scrutiny of high-multiple tech names remain the dominant forces guiding price action.

Technical structure weakens as Microsoft tests major support

The daily chart shows clear signs of deterioration. Microsoft has broken below both the 20-day and 50-day EMAs, with each now sloping downward and acting as dynamic overhead resistance. Attempts to rebound into these zones last week were met with aggressive selling, confirming a shift in control from buyers to sellers.

The Supertrend indicator flipped bearish earlier in November and has remained firmly above price ever since, reinforcing the sustained momentum shift. The stock is now drifting toward the 200-day EMA around $479, a level not tested since April’s breakout. As one of the most reliable long-term support references, it represents a critical battleground for bulls attempting to halt the decline.

The broader pattern shows a controlled sequence of lower highs and lower lows developing since the $550–$560 region. This structured retreat, rather than a sharp unwind, signals that distribution is occurring gradually. Slow, steady pressure often allows a downtrend to grind further before a true stabilization attempt materializes.

If Microsoft breaks decisively below the 200-day EMA, the next notable support sits near $465, a consolidation zone from the summer that previously acted as a platform for buying interest.

Momentum indicators show a market still under distribution

Momentum metrics reinforce the caution. RSI has slipped into the low-to-mid range after failing to hold above the 50 level earlier this month. Without bullish divergence, there is little evidence that the downtrend is tiring. Volume levels remain subdued compared to the stock's major inflection points, a sign that selling is steady but not capitulatory.

Short-term price action mirrors the broader trend. On the 30-minute chart, Microsoft continues to trade beneath the Supertrend and intraday EMAs, with every push toward the $596–$600 region rejected quickly. The DMI indicator shows the negative directional index firmly above the positive index, confirming that sellers continue to dominate intraday flows.

Bulls need a reclaim of the 50-day EMA and a Supertrend reversal before the technical tone can shift meaningfully. Without those developments, the stock remains exposed to further downside, with the 200-day EMA serving as the immediate line of defense.

Strategic Saudi cloud partnership overshadowed by market tone

Fundamentally, Microsoft’s announcement in Saudi Arabia highlights its accelerating push into sovereign-cloud infrastructure. The proposed collaboration with the Public Investment Fund and SITE aims to develop localized cloud services designed to meet national data-sovereignty requirements—an area of rising demand among governments and regulated industries.

Under more stable technical conditions, such an agreement would likely have offered support. Sovereign-cloud expansion presents a long runway for future revenue and strengthens Microsoft’s competitive position against hyperscale rivals. But current market sentiment has overshadowed even strategically meaningful developments, as high-growth tech undergoes repricing amid rising rate expectations and broader Nasdaq cooling.

Macro headwinds remain a secondary but persistent factor. The rotation out of high-multiple tech has pressured leading names across the index, and Microsoft’s decline reflects not just company-specific positioning but also sector-wide caution.

In earlier analysis, we noted that Microsoft’s failure to hold the 20-day EMA risked exposing the stock to a deeper test of the 50-day and eventually the 200-day EMA. The current decline confirms that scenario, with price now approaching the long-term average highlighted as the key pivot for trend stability.

This material may contain third-party opinions, none of the data and information on this webpage constitutes investment advice according to our Disclaimer. While we adhere to strict Editorial Integrity, this post may contain references to products from our partners.

data.title: $100,000 In The S&P 500's 5 Cheapest Healthcare Stocks, 4% Average Dividend Yield
data.description:
Hunting for the cheapest large caps in the S&P 500

In my last broad-based market article, sifting through the cheapest large-cap names of the S&P 500 (SP500)(SPY)(VOO)(IVV), I found a few trends. There were a few unique company-specific deals in Adobe (ADBE), Nike (NKE), Lululemon (LULU) that have been sold off due to their own problems and can be considered turnaround plays in need of a new direction. Those types of stocks are not always my favorite to make broad based bets on because there may be something actually wrong with the company at hand.

Then we have the sector specific trends. These more or less speak to the cyclicality of the market and the economy and may have less to say about the actual companies and more to say about where we sit in the cycle. The two trends that popped up in the screen revealed cheap deals in oil field equipment and the Health Care sector.

Screening methodology used

In many of my sector specific or broad based index value screens, I utilize Joel Greenblatt's screening methodology by using a simple quant system as described in Greenblatt's book The Little Book That Beats The Market. The easy screening methodology also comes paired with a free public screener called the Magic Formula screener. It simply hunts for stocks at a set market cap that are defined as cheap by having a high earnings yield, and of high quality by having a high return on invested capital [ROIC].

If the screener you're using does not provide metrics for return on invested capital, return on assets [ROA] or return on total capital [ROTC] can substitute. For the sake of this article and the screeners on Seeking Alpha, we will utilize FWD GAAP earnings yield and ROTC. The earnings yield is simply the inversion of the P/E ratio [taking 1/ P/E]. This gets us two numbers where the more positive, the better, whereas with P/E in general, the lower that ratio, the better. This is a popular way to backtest quantitative theory through chaining together various yields. So simply put, earnings yield + ROTC = score.

The selection group

The sample group are both above the market cap threshold for the S&P 500 [around $18 billion and up] and come from a Magic Formula screen. I isolate the Health Care sector stocks only. They are scored and then weighted in a hypothetical $100,000 portfolio by the weight of their factor-based score. So if a total score of all stocks was 100 and an individual company had a score of 10, they would have a factor weight of 10% in the model portfolio. Often times I find sector funds to be overly diversified when owning the large-cap market leaders in times of a cyclical headwind is a better idea than buying the whole sector, in my opinion. This is how I personally approach value based sector buy ins.

Year to date sector performances

Using the 11 SPDR Select Sector funds to observe year to date market trends [price return only], Health Care is no longer the worst performer of the group year to date. They are hitting a rotation trend and the Trump administration has begun to play nice with the Health Care companies offering some concessions to all of the previous attacks on the sector at the beginning of 2025 about drug costs. The compromise seems to be limiting much of the drug price reduction to medicare recipients which is a fairly small group of individuals in the grand scheme of things.

Health Care now sits middle of the pack at +10.85% as a sector year to date.

Let's take a look at the 5 large cap high scorers from my screen:

All data via Seeking Alpha

Company Sector Industry Market Cap Enterprise Value
Amgen (AMGN) Health Care Biotechnology 185.23B 230.37B
Pfizer (PFE) Health Care Pharmaceuticals 144.70B 191.95B
Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY) Health Care Pharmaceuticals 95.80B 129.98B
Gilead Sciences (GILD) Health Care Biotechnology 157.79B 172.15B
Merck & Co (MRK) Health Care Pharmaceuticals 239.34B 262.56B

The companies selected are all commonly known blue chips of the sector. Even though my screener market cap was set at $18 billion, these 5 companies all exceed $100 billion in market cap. I do like it when companies this large are trading at a discount to the market, in my opinion that adds in some additional level of security.

The scoring outcome

STOCK EARNINGS YIELD % ROTC% TOTAL SCORE WEIGHT BY FACTOR
AMGN 4 10.56 14.56 14.53%
PFE 7.7 7.04 14.74 14.70%
BMY 9 12.94 21.94 21.90%
GILD 5.4 16.14 21.54 21.50%
MRK 8.07 19.31 27.38 27.37%
total 100.16 100.00%

The cheapest of the group at the moment is Merk & Co. at a score of 27.38. Thus at this entry point Merk & Co. would get the largest chunk of investment from a factor weighted lump sum.

Rank Company Score
1 MRK 27.38
2 BMY 21.94
3 GILD 21.54
4 PFE 14.74
5 AMGN 14.56

A hypothetical $100,000 portfolio sleeve

Above is a hypothetical $100,000 weighted portfolio amongst these 5 names.

A 4% average dividend yield to boot

Stock FWD Dividend Yield Portfolio Weight Weighted Contribution
AMGN 2.77% 14.53% 0.402%
PFE 6.76% 14.70% 0.994%
BMY 5.27% 21.90% 1.155%
GILD 2.48% 21.50% 0.533%
MRK 3.36% 27.37% 0.919%
TOTAL — 100.00% 4.00%

This group is also yielding a 4% average dividend when weighted in this fashion. That would produce $4,000 per year in qualified dividend income at this entry point for long term holders.

Versus historical dividend yields

We can see that 4 out of the 5 stocks are now trading near decade high dividend yields. A great entry point for those looking for yield in a resilient sector as money market and longer-term fixed income rates are expected to drop into the future.

Earnings yield of the group versus the market

Then we come to how cheap this group is compared to the market. The market is trading at a forward P/E of 23X. If we invert this, we get an earnings yield of 4.3%. This group trades at an average earnings yield of 6.834%. That's the equivalent of an average forward P/E ratio of 14.62 [P/E = 1/earnings yield]. A significant discount to the market.

Risks and summary

This is a model portfolio for demonstration purposes only, but it gives an investor a feel for how cheap Health Care remains even after a recovery uptick in prices this year. Health care stocks, along with REITs remain my favorite sectors. REITs are not included in magic formula screens because they function differently than companies that retain earnings, whereas REITs pay out most of their earnings and borrow to grow.

Risks in buying Health Care have to do with patent cliffs [for which many of these companies have drugs that will soon be available on the generic market] and a turn in regulatory sentiment. The Trump administration and HHS Secretary Robert F. Kennedy have healthcare concerns amongst their top priorities in regulatory focus. Packaged foods seem to be at the biggest disadvantage when it comes to health related ingredient changes and the cost of goods sold rising because of it, but drug companies do rely on regulators to approve new drugs which could also be an issue. The rubber stamp may not be as liberally used as it once was.

Overall this group is large cap, cheap and most are trading near decade high dividend yields.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Mixed Messages On U.S. Jobs Will Keep The Fed Hawkish
data.description:
By James Knightley, Chief International Economist, US

The September US jobs report (delayed from its 3 October scheduled release date) is firmer than the consensus predicted at +119,000 additions to non-farm payrolls versus 51,000 expected. We imagined the risks were always skewed to the upside, given the Federal Reserve had a good idea of its contents at the October FOMC when it cut rates by 25bp but adopted a more hawkish posture on future moves. This was likely one of the triggers.

Change in private payrolls (000s)

Nonetheless, the report isn't universally strong, with 33k downward revisions to the previous two months. The breakdown shows that it is the three usual suspects that contributed virtually all the jobs: leisure & hospitality +47k, government +22k and private education & healthcare services +59k. Remember, the federal government workers who accepted deals to leave won't drop off until the October report, which will now be combined with the November report and released on 16 December.

Meanwhile, the unemployment rate rose to 4.4% from 4.3%, and wage growth rose only 0.2% month-on-month. The unemployment rate comes from a separate survey of households. This showed a 470,000 increase in the labour force, but only 251,000 found jobs, so unemployment grew by 219,000. This survey tends to be more volatile than the establishment survey of firms that generates the non-farm payrolls number.

Consumer sentiment on jobs vs change in private payrolls

Given that we won't receive any more official jobs data ahead of the 10 December FOMC meeting, and we aren't confident on the inflation release schedule, it is understandable that the market is only pricing a 35% chance of a 25bp cut. That said, the probability is up from 24% at 8:00am, ahead of the release. It looks as though the market may be focusing a little more on the unemployment rate creeping higher. It is true to say that business surveys paint a gloomier picture of the jobs market, such as the ADP report and the ISM indicators, but we are going to need to see even more weakness to get the Fed to move in three weeks' time.

Charts, such as the one above, suggest the Fed will have more work to do in 2026. Workers remain very downbeat on the jobs market, and the relationship shows that over the past 50 years, workers see and feel changes before they show up in the official data. That doesn’t bode well for upcoming jobs reports.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

data.title: Griffon: Decent Q4 And FY25 Results, But Tariff Impacts Still Linger
data.description:
Recap

Earlier this year, I covered Griffon Corporation (GFF) following their Q3 earnings call. At that time, the stock was trading for about $76 per share and trading for a small discount based on my FCF valuation model. I rated the stock a Hold as revenue had decreased significantly in Q3 for the Consumer and Professional Products segment as a result of ongoing tariff issues. Since my initial write-up, the stock has declined about 9%, while the S&P 500 (SP500) has risen by 3%.

Yesterday Griffon announced their Q4 and fiscal 2025 results. Let's take a look at the release and see if things have improved for the CPP segment, and examine the current valuation based on updated FCF numbers.

Q4 & Full-Year 2025 Earnings

Griffon announced their Q4 and full-year 2025 earnings before the market opened on Wednesday, November 19th. Overall, the earnings release was pretty positive as the quarterly numbers beat estimates on both ends. Non-GAAP EPS was $1.54 for the quarter topping the consensus by $0.03, on revenue of $662.2M which exceeded analyst expectations by almost $31M and was up minimally compared to the prior year. Fiscal year revenue shrank 4% to $2.5B from 2.6B, while an impairment charge hampered 2025 EPS numbers which came in at $1.09 per share, compared to $4.23 in FY24.

The Home and Building Products division saw quarterly revenue climb 3% YoY to more than $420M from $406.5M, as a result of product mix and favorable pricing adjustments. Additionally, demand for Commercial products was strong, which more than offset weaker demand from the Residential segment. Adjusted EBITDA was flat YoY as the increase in revenue was completely negated by a rise in material costs as well as increased wages.

For the fiscal year, revenue for the HBP segment slipped 2% overall, or $4.5M, with the Commercial division seeing a decline of $8M, or 1.1%, while Residential Repair and New Construction sales rose minimally. The adjusted EBITDA for the year shrank by 1% to $494.6M for same reasons the quarterly number was flat.

The Consumer and Professional Products segment struggled with quarterly sales declining $12M, or 4.4% year-over-year. Tariffs were the major driver here leading to an 8% drop in volume, partially offset by a beneficial price and product mix. Adjusted EBITDA for the quarter was down minimally as the company made gains in sourcing more U.S. products, despite the lower volumes.

Overall, FY25 revenue for CPP fell more than $100M, or 10%, as the tariffs effected this line of business significantly including weak demand in both the U.S. and U.K, leading to a YoY volume decrease of close to 12%. These declines were partially offset by a rise in organic sales in Australia, which also saw continued tailwinds from the 2024 Pope acquisition. Amazingly, adjusted EBITDA rose nearly 20% to $85.5M, despite the obvious struggles the CPP segment faced, as reduced administrative costs and U.S. sourcing more than offset foreign exchange headwinds.

Griffon also announced a robust 22.2% dividend increase, raising it from $0.18 to $0.22 per quarter and giving GFF a forward dividend yield 1.27%. In recent years, the company has grown its dividend at a rapid pace with its 3-year CAGR at more than 25%, while its 5 & 10-year dividend growth rates are strong too at 19.1% and 16.2%, respectively. I'd expect to see the dividend continue to increase sharply, as the company's payout ratio is below 20% leaving ample room for future dividend growth.

Management projects 2026 revenue to be flat, while adjusted EBITDA is anticipated to rise more than 10%, which follows just a 2% increase from 2024 to 2025.

The market was encouraged by the quarterly and fiscal results with shares of Griffon rising close to 7% in early trading, before ending the day up more than 3.5%.

The full earnings call can be read/listened to here.

Risks/Headwinds

Griffon delivered resilient results despite a challenging environment. The company will need to maintain discipline to sustain these promising results.

The obvious headwind facing Griffon are the tariffs, which effected the company's 2025 results. Management has made strides to reduce the effects from them, particularly in the CPP segment; however, any additional tariffs or trade disputes could exacerbate this problem.

Griffon relies on growth by acquisition as one of the main ways to expand, as many company do. Having said that, in Q3 2025 the company incurred a significant impairment charge of more than $200M related to the Hunter Fan deal. Although the company was able to absorb that impact, it does give me concern as an investor that management could overpay for future acquisitions leading to additional financial impacts.

One final risk facing Griffon, is the reliance on the housing market, which the Home and Building Products segment is strongly linked to. Any material downtown in the housing market could lead to reduced demand for HBP products and significantly effect earnings.

Valuation

The last piece of this puzzle is to determine if Griffon is trading for an attractive valuation, and as usual we'll look at it from a free cash flow perspective.

I estimate a stock's implied fair value based on its free cash flow yield and specifically how the current yield compares to the company's historical average. This helps me gauge how much investors have usually been willing to pay for a company's free cash flow and how attractive the stock looks relative to that history. The analysis is calculated on 12 years of daily historical price data capturing a rolling 5-year average FCF yield for the past 7 years. The valuation chart below compares Griffon's actual share price and the fair value range calculated using this methodology.

The main assumption behind this valuation method is that investors have paid a fair price for the stock over the past 5 years on average, but since no valuation method is perfect, there is a +/-10% margin of error applied to the fair value range.

My custom free cash flow valuation model indicates Griffon is trading for a moderate discount to its fair value, with a current price just below $70 and a fair price closer to $80. Estimating the fair value of Griffin can be difficult due to drastic shifts in the growth of their FCF. For example, the 5-year FCF CAGR is just below 30%, while the 3-year CAGR is above 140%, so this volatility is something investors should take into account.

The long-term expected rate of return for Griffon was previously 13.6%, and is up marginally since my initial coverage to 14.6%. The components of this estimate are a forward dividend yield of 1.27%, a return to fair value factor of 2.47%, and an expected earnings growth rate of 10.87%.

Final Thoughts

To conclude, the stock has struggled since my initial coverage, declining around 9%. The HBP segment continues to see decent results, while the CPP segment has been faced with glaring impacts from tariffs. The company raised its dividend by 22%, indicating management's confidence to in their current and future results. Griffon's valuation is more appealing than it was previously, and I think investors should consider this stock following these results and a healthy dividend bump.

Ultimately, I am going to maintain a Hold rating, as the company is still dealing with lingering effects from the tariffs. It'll take some time before those can be mitigated in a considerable way.

data.title: Nuclear Projects Don't Have To Be Late And Overbudget
data.description:
By Stacey Morris, CFA

One of the pushbacks to nuclear power is its high, upfront capital costs, which can be potentially exacerbated by cost overruns and delays. This was certainly the case for the last large reactors built in the U.S. in Georgia (Vogtle 3 and 4), and the reactors currently under construction in the UK have also seen challenges.

While the nuclear projects likely to end up in the newsfeeds of U.S. investors have faced setbacks, other countries have seen better execution on large reactor projects. Admittedly, countries like China benefit from standardization and repetition with multiple projects under construction. However, the United Arab Emirates (UAE) provides an example of a new entrant to nuclear power that saw very strong execution on building reactors in recent years.

The UAE Example

Historically reliant on fossil fuels, the UAE turned to nuclear energy to secure emission-free power to fuel economic growth. Its four-reactor Barakah plant proves that nuclear power projects can be delivered on schedule and on budget. The first unit took eight years from the initial concrete pour to the start of operations, and efficiencies were realized with the subsequent units. For context, Vogtle 3 in the U.S. took 14 years to complete and was roughly seven years behind schedule.

Rigorous planning, global collaboration, and thorough budgeting are some of the key elements of the UAE’s approach that contributed to its overall success. Potential project partners were evaluated for a year by a team of 75 nuclear experts from around the world.

Ultimately, Korea Electric Power Corporation (KEPCO) was chosen to design, build, and support the operations of the Barakah Plant. Nuclear reactors that came on-line in 2016 and 2019 in South Korea using the same technology (APR 1400) served as reference plants for Barakah. KEPCO is a constituent of the Range Nuclear Renaissance Index (NUKZX).

Why do some projects struggle?

Nuclear projects can be challenging, as they’re often first-of-a-kind facilities, deploying a design that has not been used before. This can understandably create difficulties. In practicality, the third or fourth version is easier than the first, and later iterations benefit from prior learnings. Regulations can also contribute to delays and cost overruns, with nuclear projects subject to significant oversight.

Nuclear development becomes better and more efficient with repetition. Certainly, there are efficiency gains from the first to the second or fourth unit on the same site. More broadly, multiple projects under construction in a country would drive more robust supply chains and a stronger workforce. As the U.S. seeks to triple nuclear power capacity by 2050, these are key areas that need support and have been an emphasis in executive orders from the Trump administration (read more).

Nuclear isn’t simple, but has clear advantages

Admittedly, nuclear power plants are costly and time-intensive to build, but they also generate zero-emission, reliable baseload power for decades. As of April 2025, the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission had approved 12 reactors to operate for 80 years, and was reviewing applications from an additional five reactors to extend their licenses to 80 years. Upfront costs are more manageable when viewed through the lens of 80 or more years of operation. For context, the life span for less-reliable solar panels or wind turbines may be 20-30 years.

Nuclear is also not unique in taking years to develop. Consider oil and gas, where production depletes over time compared to nuclear’s steady output. According to the International Energy Agency, it typically takes 20 years between when exploration licenses are granted and hydrocarbons are actually produced.

Bottom Line

Nuclear power projects are expensive and take time. However, their longevity of operation, reliability, and emission-free power generation represent distinct advantages. The UAE provides a prime example of solid execution on large reactors, proving nuclear projects do not have to be plagued with delays and cost overruns.

NUKZX is the underlying index for the Range Nuclear Renaissance Index ETF (NUKZ).

Disclosure: © VettaFi LLC 2025. All rights reserved. This material has been prepared and/or issued by VettaFi LLC ("VettaFi") and/or one of its consultants or affiliates. It is provided as general information only and should not be taken as investment advice. Employees of VettaFi are prohibited from owning individual MLPs. For more information on VettaFi, visit www.vettafi.com.

Original Post
